turns.question: what is a sailplane or glider
turns.answerText: A glider is an aircraft that relies entirely on aerodynamics to fly, functioning without an engine. It is supported in flight by the dynamic reaction of the air against its lifting surfaces. While the terms are often used interchangeably, enthusiasts make a technical distinction: a glider is any unpowered aircraft (including hang gliders and paragliders), while a sailplane is a highly refined glider designed for soaring — efficient enough to gain altitude by harnessing rising air currents rather than just descending.
turns.answerHtml:
A glider is an aircraft that relies entirely on aerodynamics to fly, functioning without an engine.

  • Glider: any unpowered aircraft — hang gliders, paragliders, WWII troop gliders, even paper airplanes.
  • Sailplane: a highly refined glider designed for soaring, efficient enough to gain altitude from rising air.

turns.question: is there a 3d printed sailplane
turns.answerText: The short answer is yes, but currently only in the world of unmanned, radio-controlled (RC) models and drones. Hobbyists print lightweight wings and fuselages in materials like LW-PLA (lightweight foaming PLA). Full-size, human-carrying 3D printed sailplanes do not yet exist due to material strength and certification requirements.
turns.answerHtml: The short answer is yes, but currently only in the world of unmanned, radio-controlled (RC) models and drones. Hobbyists print lightweight wings and fuselages in materials like LW-PLA (lightweight foaming PLA).

turns.question: video links to some of these projects ... are they for full size or rc size planes?
turns.answerText: To answer your question directly: these are all strictly RC (radio-controlled) scale models. At this time, there are no full-size 3D printed sailplanes certified to carry a human pilot. The projects you'll find online demonstrate impressive engineering at model scale, often with wingspans of 1.5 to 3 meters.
turns.answerHtml: To answer your question directly: these are all strictly RC (radio-controlled) scale models. At this time, there are no full-size 3D printed sailplanes certified to carry a human pilot.

turns.question: is there open source software to help find thermals?
turns.answerText: Yes, there is a thriving open-source community dedicated to soaring. Depending on whether you want software to help you plan flights or analyze them, options include XCSoar (a tactical glide computer), LK8000, and Top Hat. These tools predict thermal locations using terrain, weather data, and live sensor input.
turns.answerHtml:
Yes, there is a thriving open-source community dedicated to soaring:

  • XCSoar — a tactical glide computer.
  • LK8000 and Top Hat — flight planning and analysis.

turns.question: what is longest flight time for a sailplane uav
turns.answerText: Because 'sailplane UAV' can refer to a few different types of aircraft, the record depends entirely on whether the drone is purely solar/soaring powered or assisted. Solar-powered High-Altitude Pseudo-Satellites like the Airbus Zephyr have stayed aloft for weeks at a time, with one flight exceeding 64 days.
turns.answerHtml: The record depends on whether the drone is purely solar/soaring powered or assisted. Solar-powered High-Altitude Pseudo-Satellites like the Airbus Zephyr have stayed aloft for weeks — one flight exceeded 64 days.

turns.question: are the haps towed into the stratosphere?
turns.answerText: No, High-Altitude Pseudo-Satellites (HAPS) are not towed into the stratosphere. Because they are built with extreme weight restrictions to stay aloft for months at a time, they are incredibly fragile. Instead, they launch from the ground and reach the stratosphere using their own power or buoyancy.
turns.answerHtml: No, High-Altitude Pseudo-Satellites (HAPS) are not towed into the stratosphere. They are too fragile. Instead, they launch from the ground and reach the stratosphere using their own solar-electric power or buoyancy.
